did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) architecture (CNSVM) for feature extraction and classification of near-infrared hyperspectral signatures to differentiate between healthy skin, Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'Research Paper' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving near-infrared hyperspectral imaging of 125 patients, the development and training of a hybrid CNN-SVM model (CNSVM), and the reporting of new results including accuracy, F1 scores, and RMSE metrics for classifying skin lesions.

title: Using computational learning for non-melanoma skin cancer and actinic keratosis near-infrared hyperspectral signature classification
brief_summary: This study evaluated the use of near-infrared hyperspectral imaging combined with a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network and Support Vector Machine (CNSVM) architecture to classify non-melanoma skin cancers and actinic keratosis. The dataset comprised hyperspectral signatures from 125 patients, including those with Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Actinic Keratosis, as well as healthy skin controls. The primary evaluation approach involved binary and categorical classification tasks using metrics such as accuracy, F1 scores, and RMSE. The model achieved general accuracies over 80%, with specific tasks reaching over 90%, and demonstrated particular strength in detecting Actinic Keratosis. However, differentiating between the two malignant lesion types proved more difficult, and transfer learning from shorter wavelengths was unsuccessful. The results suggest that near-infrared hyperspectral imaging is promising for NMSC identification, though further research is needed to improve algorithm robustness for clinical application.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the dataset of 125 patients (n=66 patients) and 8250 hyperspectral signatures (n=1980 samples). Classified against healthy skin, SCC, and AK.

Included in the dataset of 125 patients (n=42 patients) and 8250 hyperspectral signatures (n=1260 samples). Classified against healthy skin, BCC, and AK. Differentiation from BCC was noted as difficult.
Included in the dataset of 125 patients (n=17 patients) and 8250 hyperspectral signatures (n=510 samples). Considered a premalignant condition. The model showed best results in detecting AK.
Included in the dataset of 125 patients and 8250 hyperspectral signatures (n=4500 samples). Used as the negative control/comparator class in binary and categorical classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Healthy Skin, Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) derived from near-infrared hyperspectral signatures.
all_task_types.details: The study performs both binary classification (e.g., H vs BCC, H vs AK, SCC vs BCC) and categorical classification (multi-class) to differentiate between healthy skin and various non-melanoma skin cancers/precancers. The model predicts discrete class labels. Metrics reported include accuracy, F1 score, precision, recall, and AUROC. While RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) is reported as a loss metric for the classification task, the primary output is class prediction, not a continuous numeric outcome.

datasets_used.name: Avila Healthcare Complex Hyperspectral Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset derived from near-infrared hyperspectral imaging of 125 patients at the Avila Healthcare Complex (Avila, Castilla y Leon, Spain) between March and July 2022. The data consists of hyperspectral signatures obtained using a Headwall Hyperspec NIR imaging sensor (pushbroom linear camera, 900-1700 nm range, 166 channels). The dataset is accessible online at https://github.com/LACourtenay/Deteccthia_Skin_Cancer_Project. It includes manually defined Regions of Interest (ROIs) for lesions and healthy skin, with pixel sampling performed via Python script. Diagnoses were confirmed via histopathology.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid Convolutional Neural Network and Support Vector Machine (CNSVM) models for binary and categorical classification tasks.
use.validation: Used for validation of the CNSVM models (accuracy and loss curves reported for training and validation data).
use.testing: Used for testing the CNSVM models to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, F1, RMSE, etc.).
use.other: Used to test transfer learning from networks trained on shorter wavelengths (VNIR) to the NIR region.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curve

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Parameter optimisation for the CNSVM models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, AUROC, and RMSE.

transfer_learning_binary_median.notes: Highest confusion rates observed when differentiating between SCC and AK, followed by SCC and BCC. Loss rates were higher for these cases except H vs SCC. AUPRC values dropped significantly, especially for SCC vs AK.

Avila Healthcare Complex Hyperspectral D.notes: The best results were obtained when detecting Actinic Keratosis. Differentiation between the two types of malignant lesions (BCC and SCC) was noted to be more difficult. Transfer learning was unsuccessful in improving training. The ReLU activation function produced the best results. Training took on average ≈ 2.5 min for the base CNN and ≈ 5 min for SVM tuning. Algorithms required 2.71 GB of RAM.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning attempted from a CNSVM trained on Visible-Near-Infrared (VNIR) data (Courtenay et al. [22]); weights transferred only to dense layers, retrained for 400 epochs. Standard training used no pretraining.
architecture_used.input_size: Input tensor of shape ℝ^97 (97 hyperspectral channels derived from 4 wavelength windows: 900.7-1085.4 nm, 1109.1-1208.5 nm, 1237.0-1322.2 nm, and 1383.8-1454.8 nm).

architecture_details.inception_modules: Two parallel Inception modules. Each module contains parallel 1D convolutional layers with receptive field sizes of 1x1, 1x3, and 1x5. Filters have padding and stride of 1. A 3x1 max-pooling layer is included. Batch normalization applied after each convolutional layer.
architecture_details.dense_layers: 4 fully connected dense layers following the final Inception module. Neuron densities: 200, 150, 100, and 50.
architecture_details.regularization: L2 regularizer with coefficient 0.0001 applied to convolutional layers.

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Cyclic Learning Rate (CLR) with Triangular2 function (Base alpha = 0.0001, Max alpha = 0.01, step size = 8).

svm_tuning.hyperparameter_optimization: Bayesian Optimisation Algorithms (BOA) with Tree of Parzen Estimator (TPE), 100 iterations, expected improvement selection function.

training_details.transfer_learning_details: Dense layers of pre-trained model unfrozen; convolutional layers frozen. Retrained for 400 epochs.

classification_or_output_head.description: The final activation layer of the dense neural network is replaced by an SVM. Features are extracted by passing hyperspectral signatures through the trained CNN portion.

preprocessing_techniques: Wavelength window selection: Robust statistics used to identify optimal wavelength windows (900.7-1085.4 nm, 1109.1-1208.5 nm, 1237.0-1322.2 nm, and 1383.8-1454.8 nm) from the original 150 channels, resulting in a final input tensor of 97 channels.
Region of Interest (ROI) definition: Manually defined ROIs for lesions and healthy skin were used to extract hyperspectral signatures.
Pixel sampling: Hyperspectral signatures were sampled from the defined ROIs via a Python script.
Batch Normalization: Applied after each convolutional layer in the CNN architecture.
L2 Regularization: Applied to convolutional layers with a coefficient of 0.0001.
conclusions: The Near-Infrared region of the electromagnetic spectrum (900.6–1454.8 nm) is highly useful for the identification and study of non-melanoma type skin lesions.

The hybrid Convolutional Neural Network and Support Vector Machine (CNSVM) architecture is a promising tool for distinguishing Non-Melanoma Skin Cancers (NMSC) and Actinic Keratosis from healthy skin, achieving general accuracies over 80% and specific task accuracies over 90%.
The model demonstrated the best performance in detecting Actinic Keratosis (AK).

Differentiation between the two malignant lesion types, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), proved difficult, with higher confusion rates and lower performance metrics compared to other binary classification tasks.
Transfer learning from networks trained on shorter wavelengths (VNIR) was unsuccessful in improving the training or performance of the algorithms for NIR data.

Binary classification is currently the most effective approach for comparing different groups of skin lesions using this method, as categorical classification resulted in misleadingly high accuracy rates with very low precision or recall values.
Further research is required to develop more robust algorithms that can minimize the impact of noise in hyperspectral datasets before clinical application is feasible.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center dataset from the Ávila Healthcare Complex in Spain, consisting of 125 patients, which restricts generalizability and raises concerns about population bias (majority fair-skinned, elderly median age 79).

Differentiation between the two malignant lesion types, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), proved difficult, with lower performance metrics (e.g., 0.84 accuracy in binary classification) compared to distinguishing lesions from healthy skin.

Transfer learning from networks trained on shorter wavelengths (VNIR) was unsuccessful and resulted in decreased performance, indicating that pre-training on different spectral domains may not be beneficial for this specific NIR task.

Categorical (multi-class) classification yielded misleadingly high overall accuracy (0.74) but very low precision and recall for specific classes (e.g., AK precision 0.52, recall 0.38), highlighting the risk of relying solely on accuracy metrics for imbalanced multi-class problems.

The dataset contains significant class imbalance (Healthy Skin n=4500 vs. AK n=510), which necessitates careful metric selection (F1, AUPRC) rather than accuracy alone, as noted by the authors' use of Shannon’s entropy to assess balance.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics, limiting the statistical rigor of the comparative claims.

The study acknowledges that NIR hyperspectral data is noisier than VNIR data, and further research is needed to develop more robust algorithms to minimize noise impact before clinical application is feasible.

Diagnoses were confirmed via histopathology, which is the gold standard, but the initial visual inspection was subjective, potentially introducing bias in the initial lesion localization or ROI definition.
